title: What is an interface
instruction:
An interface defines a set of method signatures. Any type that has those methods satisfies the interface automatically — no declaration, no `implements` keyword.

Think of a USB port. It does not care if you plug in a mouse, a keyboard, or a flash drive. As long as the plug fits the shape (the interface), it works.

Run this. `Animal` is an interface with one method: `Speak() string`. Both `Dog` and `Cat` have that method, so both satisfy `Animal`.
successMessage: makeNoise accepts any Animal — it does not care if it is a Dog or a Cat. Same function, different types, different results. That is polymorphism.

title: Define and satisfy an interface
instruction:
Define a `Shape` interface with one method: `Area() float64`.

Then add `Area()` methods to both `Rectangle` and `Triangle`. The compiler will tell you if you missed anything.

Finally, write a `printArea(s Shape)` function and call it with both shapes.
hint: type Shape interface { Area() float64 }  func (r Rectangle) Area() float64 { return r.Width * r.Height }  func (t Triangle) Area() float64 { return 0.5 * t.Base * t.Height }

codeChecks.message: Define the Shape interface: type Shape interface { ... }
The interface must require Area() float64.
Add Area() method to Rectangle.
Add Area() method to Triangle.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Rectangle and Triangle are completely different types — but both satisfy Shape. printArea works with both without knowing which one it has.

title: Interfaces with multiple methods
instruction:
An interface can require any number of methods. A type must implement all of them to satisfy the interface.

Add `Perimeter() float64` to the `Shape` interface. Then add `Perimeter()` methods to both `Rectangle` and `Circle`. Update `describe` to print both.
hint: func (r Rectangle) Perimeter() float64 { return 2 * (r.Width + r.Height) }  func (c Circle) Perimeter() float64 { return 2 * 3.14159 * c.Radius }

codeChecks.message: Add Perimeter() float64 to the Shape interface.
Add Perimeter() to Rectangle.
Add Perimeter() to Circle.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Both methods, both types. If you add a method to the interface but forget to add it to a type, the compiler tells you immediately — no runtime surprises.

title: A slice of interfaces
instruction:
Because all three shapes satisfy `Shape`, you can store them in a `[]Shape` and treat them uniformly.

Add a `Circle{Radius: 3}` to the shapes slice, then loop through and call `describe` on each.

codeChecks.message: Add Circle{Radius: 3} to the shapes slice.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Three different types in one slice, one loop, one function. This is the power of interfaces — write the loop once, it works for every type that satisfies Shape.

title: The Stringer interface
instruction:
You already used this in the Methods chapter without knowing what it was.

`fmt.Stringer` is a standard library interface:
```
type Stringer interface {
    String() string
}
```

If your type has a `String() string` method, `fmt.Println` calls it automatically.

Add a `String() string` method to `Point` that returns `"(3, 7)"` format. Then just `fmt.Println(p)` and watch it use your method.
hint: func (p Point) String() string { return fmt.Sprintf("(%d, %d)", p.X, p.Y) }

codeChecks.message: Add String() string method to Point.
Use fmt.Sprintf inside String() to format the output.
Replace the TODO comment with your method.

successMessage: fmt.Println saw that Point implements Stringer and called your String() method automatically. This is implicit interface satisfaction — no registration, no declaration.

title: Interface as function parameter
instruction:
Interfaces shine when used as function parameters. The function does not care about the concrete type — only that it has the right methods.

`io.Writer` is the most famous example. `fmt.Fprintf` takes any `io.Writer` — a file, a network connection, a buffer, anything.

Run this. `logMessage` accepts any `io.Writer`. We pass `os.Stdout` — the terminal. The function works the same regardless of where the output goes.
successMessage: logMessage does not know it is writing to a terminal. It just knows it has a Writer. Swap os.Stdout for a file or a network socket and the function does not change at all.

title: Type assertion
instruction:
Sometimes you have an interface value and you need to access the concrete type underneath.

`concrete, ok := interfaceVal.(ConcreteType)`

If the underlying type is `ConcreteType`, `ok` is `true` and `concrete` has the concrete value. If not, `ok` is `false` and `concrete` is the zero value.

Run this. Then add a type assertion to check if `s` is a `Circle` and print its radius if it is.
hint: if c, ok := s.(Circle); ok { fmt.Println("Circle radius:", c.Radius) }

codeChecks.message: Add a type assertion: if c, ok := s.(Circle); ok { ... }
Print the Circle's Radius.
Replace the TODO comment with your assertion.

successMessage: The two-value assertion (value, ok) is the safe version. Never use the one-value version s.(Circle) without ok — it panics if the type is wrong.

title: Type switch
instruction:
When you need to handle multiple concrete types from an interface, a type switch is cleaner than chaining type assertions.

```
switch v := i.(type) {
case int:
    fmt.Println("int:", v)
case string:
    fmt.Println("string:", v)
default:
    fmt.Println("unknown")
}
```

Add a `case Triangle` to the type switch that prints the base and height.
hint: case Triangle: fmt.Printf("Triangle: base=%.0f height=%.0f\n", v.Base, v.Height)

codeChecks.message: Add case Triangle: to the type switch.
Print the Triangle's Base and Height using v.
Replace the TODO comment with your case.

successMessage: Type switch: one construct, multiple types, zero repetition. Much cleaner than three consecutive if-else type assertions.

title: The empty interface
instruction:
An interface with no methods — `interface{}` — is satisfied by every type in Go. It is the "accept anything" type.

In modern Go (1.18+) it has an alias: `any`.

Used in `fmt.Println`, maps of mixed values, and anywhere you genuinely need to accept any type.

Run this. Then add a call to `printAnything` with a `[]int{1, 2, 3}`.

codeChecks.message: Call printAnything([]int{1, 2, 3}).
Replace the TODO comment with your call.

successMessage: any accepts everything. The %T verb reveals the real type at runtime. Use any sparingly — specific interfaces are always better — but it is essential for generic utility functions like Println.

title: Composing interfaces
instruction:
Interfaces can embed other interfaces. The composed interface requires all methods from all embedded interfaces.

```
type ReadWriter interface {
    Reader  // embeds Reader (requires Read)
    Writer  // embeds Writer (requires Write)
}
```

This is how Go's standard library is built. `io.ReadWriter` is just `io.Reader` + `io.Writer`.

Run this. `Saver` embeds both `Storer` and `Retriever`. `MemoryStore` must implement all three methods.
successMessage: MemoryStore satisfies Saver by implementing both Store and Retrieve. Interface composition lets you build precise, minimal contracts — the Go standard library uses this pattern throughout.

title: Implicit satisfaction — no implements keyword
instruction:
This is the most important thing about Go interfaces.

In Java or C#, you write `class Dog implements Animal`. In Go — nothing. If `Dog` has the right methods, it satisfies `Animal` automatically.

This means you can satisfy an interface defined in someone else's package without importing it — and you can retrofit existing types to satisfy new interfaces.

Run this. `LoudDog` is defined here. `fmt.Stringer` is defined in the standard library. `LoudDog` satisfies it just by having the right method — no import of `fmt` in the type definition needed.
expectedOutput: WOOF! I AM Rex AND I AM VERY LOUD!
The dog says: WOOF! I AM Rex AND I AM VERY LOUD!
successMessage: LoudDog never said it implements fmt.Stringer. It just has the method. Go connected them automatically. This is duck typing with compile-time safety — the best of both worlds.

title: Fix the broken interface
instruction:
The code below tries to use `Speaker` interface with `Robot`. But it does not compile — `Robot` is missing something.

Fix it so the program prints correctly.
hint: Robot needs a Speak() string method. Add: func (r Robot) Speak() string { return "Beep boop, I am " + r.Model }

codeChecks.message: Add Speak() string method to Robot.
Robot's Speak() should return a string starting with "Beep boop".

successMessage: One method added, the interface satisfied, the compiler happy. This is the error you will see most often when working with interfaces: missing method on concrete type.

title: Build a notification system
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a notification system:

1. Define a `Notifier` interface with one method: `Send(message string) string`
2. Create three types: `EmailNotifier` (has `To` field), `SMSNotifier` (has `Phone` field), `PushNotifier` (has `DeviceID` field)
3. Each `Send` returns a formatted confirmation string:
   - Email: `"Email sent to alex@email.com: Hello"`
   - SMS: `"SMS sent to +1234567890: Hello"`
   - Push: `"Push sent to device-abc123: Hello"`
4. Create a `[]Notifier` slice with all three, loop through and print the result of each `Send("Hello")`
hint: func (e EmailNotifier) Send(msg string) string { return fmt.Sprintf("Email sent to %s: %s", e.To, msg) }

codeChecks.message: Define a Notifier interface.
The Notifier interface must have Send(message string) string.
Define an EmailNotifier struct.
Define an SMSNotifier struct.
Define a PushNotifier struct.
Create a []Notifier slice.
Loop through the notifiers with range.
Call .Send("Hello") on each notifier.

successMessage: Three types, one interface, one loop. Email, SMS, and Push — completely different implementations, completely interchangeable. Add a fourth notifier type tomorrow and the loop does not change. That is the power of interfaces.
